post.title: Constitutional Bench clarifies the test on equal pay for equal work
post.excerpt: The Supreme Court's five-judge bench has, in a rare unanimous ruling, redrawn the contours of Article 39(d). Here's what changes for public-sector employers and the government's salary review mechanism.
post.content:
The Supreme Court of India has handed down a 187-page ruling that settles a question that has divided High Courts for over a decade. The bench — comprising the Chief Justice and four senior puisne judges — read down Section 16 of the Industrial Disputes Act for narrow application, and articulated a fresh, three-part test for claims of equal pay for equal work in the public sector.

What the bench decided

Article 39(d) is a directive principle, but the Court held it informs the equality guarantee under Article 14 and Article 16. The test articulated in Randhir Singh v. Union of India, AIR 1973 SC 1461, stands modified. The Court has now laid out three cumulative conditions that a claimant must satisfy.

First, the work compared must be substantially similar in nature, responsibility, and skill. The earlier formulation, which extended to "reasonably comparable" work, has been narrowed.

Second, the comparator must be in the same employer. Cross-employer comparisons under the same Government remain available only where there is a direct functional or organisational link.

Third, the differential must be irrational on the face of the record — that is, unjustifiable on any reasonable governmental policy ground.

The directive principles inform the equality clause; they do not replace it. Section 16 of the IDA cannot be read as a charter for indiscriminate parity claims.

Why the change

For practitioners, the most consequential portion of the judgment is paragraphs 122 to 148, where the Court reviews the litigation history of equal-pay claims since 1973. The Court records that of 412 reported decisions, 268 turned on the lack of a clear test. The new framework is intended to bring discipline to the field.

What about temporary employees?

The judgment expressly preserves the principle in State of Punjab v. Jagjit Singh, (2017) 1 SCC 148, that daily-wage and temporary employees performing identical duties are entitled to minimum pay-scale of the regular post. This is treated as a separate principle of human dignity, not a comparative-equality claim.

Government's response

The Department of Personnel and Training is expected to issue a clarificatory office memorandum within thirty days. Practitioners should watch for amendments to the Central Civil Services (Revised Pay) Rules, which the Court has flagged as in need of harmonisation.

Practical takeaways

  • Pleadings must now disclose, in detail, the comparator's job description, responsibilities, and reporting lines.
  • Affidavits in opposition will need to articulate the policy basis for any differential.
  • The earlier shortcut of citing Article 39(d) without comparator-level evidence is closed.
  • Pending matters before High Courts will need to be re-cast in light of the new test.

The full judgment is available on the Supreme Court website. For Indian counsel preparing pleadings under the new framework, see also Article 14 of the Constitution, which the Court draws on heavily in paragraphs 89 to 102.

node.name: Aanya Mehra
node.description: Aanya Mehra covers the Supreme Court for Insight Lawyer. She previously practiced commercial litigation at the Delhi High Court and clerked at the Supreme Court of India.
